scenario: You have a conscious casualty with a gunshot wound to the upper thigh, heavy bleeding, no incoming fire. Put these actions in the correct order.

items.text: Apply tourniquet 2–3 inches above the wound
Note the time of tourniquet application
Pack the wound if tourniquet is ineffective

explanation: Under fire: move to cover first. Then hemorrhage control is the priority — tourniquet high and tight. Always mark the time. Pack if the tourniquet alone doesn't control bleeding. Airway comes after hemorrhage when not in immediate danger.

scenario: You have a severe laceration to your right forearm near the elbow with arterial bleeding (spurting). You have a CAT tourniquet. You must apply it one-handed to your own arm. Put these steps in correct order.

items.text: Thread the strap through the buckle and pull tight, routing proximal to the wound
Position the tourniquet 2-3 inches proximal (toward the heart) from the wound
Twist the windlass rod until bleeding completely stops
Secure the windlass with the locking clip or hook-and-loop
Write the time of application on the white TQ label or on your skin with a marker

explanation: Position before tightening — 2-3 inches proximal to the wound, never over a joint. Tighten the strap as much as possible before touching the windlass — the strap does most of the compression work. Twist the windlass until bleeding stops completely — partial application that slows but doesn't stop bleeding is dangerous (it blocks venous return without stopping arterial flow, accelerating blood loss). Secure the windlass — an unsecured windlass can unwind, causing rebleed. Always mark the time — tourniquet time determines the evacuation priority and guides the receiving physician on nerve/vascular damage windows. Never remove the tourniquet in the field once applied.
